coredata.dc:title: Thermochemistry of nano-phased titanium dioxides relevant to energy application: A Review
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yaowen
Feng, Tao
Li, Xinbo
Li, Liping
coredata.dc:description: Polymorphs of titanium dioxides have found extensive applications in energy fields such as photocatalytic hydrogen generation and batteries. Further research on the thermochemistry of titanium dioxides should be conducted to attain a more detailed understanding of correlations among the steps of synthesis-structure-properties-performance paradigm for achieving controlled functionality. In this review, we briefly highlight important developments in polymorph structures, experimental accesses, stabilities, and thermodynamic data for nano-phased TiO2. We emphasis the important roles of surface hydration and structural symmetry on the stability of TiO2 in the context of photocatalysis and lithium-ions batteries. Because analogues of TiO2 phases have many important applications, the relevant results summarized in this work may have broad implications for the prediction of functionalities from the viewpoint of thermochemistry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure diagrams of (a) rutile, (b) anatase, (c) brookite, (d)TiO2-B, and (e)TiO2 H polymorphs for TiO2.
Raman spectra of anatase, TiO2-B, brookite and rutile. The data are obtained from references [32; 33] and experiment, respectively.
Roadmap for synthesis of five TiO2 polymorph structures.
Roadmap of TiO2 for recent thermodynamics research.
Schematic of titanium dioxides for applications in photocatalysis and lithium batteries.
Lattice data for five polymorphs of TiO2.
Synthesis, structure, and morphology of titanium dioxides.

head.ce:title: Thermochemistry of nano-phased titanium dioxides relevant to energy application: A Review

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Lab of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Materials Science and Engineering, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen, Guangdong 518055, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymorphs of titanium dioxides have found extensive applications in energy fields such as photocatalytic hydrogen generation and batteries. Further research on the thermochemistry of titanium dioxides should be conducted to attain a more detailed understanding of correlations among the steps of synthesis-structure-properties-performance paradigm for achieving controlled functionality. In this review, we briefly highlight important developments in polymorph structures, experimental accesses, stabilities, and thermodynamic data for nano-phased TiO2. We emphasis the important roles of surface hydration and structural symmetry on the stability of TiO2 in the context of photocatalysis and lithium-ions batteries. Because analogues of TiO2 phases have many important applications, the relevant results summarized in this work may have broad implications for the prediction of functionalities from the viewpoint of thermochemistry.

ce:section.ce:para: Finding alternative clean energy and developing new energy technology rely on advanced energy materials [1; 2], which becomes the driven force for fundamental research and practical prospective in solving energy crisis, one of the big concerns for human society [3; 4]. Metal oxides have been an important class of functional materials in many energy-related fields [5; 6]. For instance, layered transition metal oxides, represented by LiCoO2, are popularly used as anode materials in lithium batteries [7], while spinel oxides have shown excellent catalytic activity in OER/ORR reactions [8], and perovskite oxides have been promising electrode materials for solid oxide fuel cells [9; 10]. Majority of these material are multi-element oxide and diverse phases, which put somewhat uncertainties in understanding the structure-property relationships.

TiO2 is a prototype oxide that deserves investigations, since (i) TiO2 has a rather simple composition, which allows one to get a clear picture about the structure-property relationships with implications for other multi-component oxides; (ii) TiO2 has polymorphs (like anatase, rutile, brookite and so on) with distinct properties. These polymorphs showing different symmetry and linkages of building blocks, provides the opportunities in finely tuning the properties. (iii) TiO2 has plenty of analogues with many applications. To name a few, SnO2 is isostructural to rutile-type TiO2, which has been used in perovskite solar cells [11] and lithium-ion batteries with a relatively high theoretical capacity [12]. For anatase, transition metal (like cobalt) doping leads to dilute semiconductor with Curie temperature above 400 K [13; 14; 15]; and (iv) TiO2 is featured by low cost, non-toxicity and long-term stability [16] with potentials in acquisition of hydrogen energy, degradation of organic pollutants and lithium-ions batteries [17; 18; 19]. Thermodynamic properties directly determine the stability and therefore the materials performance for applications. However, several contradictory findings have emerged during thermodynamic studies of TiO2, in particular at nanoscale [20; 21]. Therefore, one needs well synthesized and characterized TiO2 with different phases or particle sizes so as to predict more high-performance energy oxide materials.

In this review, we focus on polymorph and experimental thermochemistry of TiO2 nanocrystal, and much less emphasis is placed on high-quality sample synthesis and applications for which several good reviews are available [4; 16; 19; 22]. This review has a hope to well explain many of the initially contradictory findings [21; 23; 24]for a correct understanding of the phase transition, phase stability, surface free energy and heat capacity of TiO2 nanocrystals and other types of oxides.

Titanium dioxides have shown many types of phases, depending on dimension, temperature and pressures. In particular, at ambient, these oxides exhibit three main phase structures, brookite, anatase, and rutile. For these polymorphs, each Ti atom is surrounded by six oxygen atoms, forming different distorted octahedra.

Brookite TiO2 has an orthorhombic structure with a space group of Pbca. Building blocks of Ti-O6 octahedra are distributed along c-axis direction to form a serrated chain. In such structure each octahedron shares an edge with three surrounding octahedra, and Ti atom locates slightly off-center at the octahedral center position. Brookite is one of the most complicated phase structures among polymorphs of TiO2, there are few studies on brookite in literature comparing other polymorphs [25]. The access to phase pure brookite is rater hard and challenging, and will be described in the next section.

Different from brookite TiO2, anatase TiO2 has a tetragonal structure with a space group of I41/amd. Building blocks of Ti-O6 octahedra are connected by two pairs of opposite shared edges and four co-apex angles, thus six titanium dioxide molecules are included in each unit cell. Such structure is not thermodynamic stable. Anatase TiO2 irreversibly changes to a rutile phase after calcination at high temperatures [26].

Rutile TiO2 is a thermodynamic stable phase [27]. It also crystallizes in a tetragonal structure, but with a space group of P42/mnm. In rutile TiO2, each octahedron is connected to ten surrounding octahedra, and the building blocks of octahedra are arranged along c-axis sharing common edges and the other directions connected with vertex.

Other polymorphs (like TiO2-B and TiO2 H) of TiO2 could be arbitrarily obtained and exist stably at ambient conditions [28; 29]. TiO2-B crystallizes in a monoclinic structure (space group C2/m), which is significantly different from those of brookite, anatase, and rutile. Comparatively, TiO2 H has a tetragonal structure (space group I4/m), similar to anatase and rutile.

Comparisons of cell parameters for these polymorphs of TiO2 are shown in Table 1 . The space group symmetries for brookite and TiO2-B are lower than those of rutile, anatase, and TiO2 H. Rutile TiO2 has a smallest cell volume of 62.42 Å3, approximately five times smaller than that of the largest cell volume (306.09Å3) of TiO2 H. TiO2 H has a lowest density at 3.47 g•cm−3. Even though these poymorph have the same molecular formula, the differences in density, cell volume and crystal structure are apparent, indicating the diversity and complexity of the roles that titanium dioxides may play in their application.

These TiO2 polymorphs are often identified by X-ray diffraction (a detection limit of 0.5%). Usually, high quality samples are required for studies on polymorph-dependent thermochemistry and many other performance [23]. In this regard, Raman spectroscopy is an effective tool in detecting coexisting phases in the studied samples [30]. Fig. 2 summarizes the Raman spectra ever reported in literature for TiO2 of different polymorphs [31; 32]. Obviously, all these polymorphs show distinct Raman data, and thus the phase purity of TiO2 can be correctly examined by Raman data comparison. For instance, the appearance of once two weak Raman signals at 395 and 514 cm−1 are observed, the examined TiO2-B sample, should contain a small amount of anatase phase (Figs. 1
, 4, 5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental accesses to polymorphs of titanium dioxides
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3
illustrates the roadmap for synthesis of five TiO2 polymorph structures. Among all polymorph titanium dioxides, brookite phase could be one of the hard one to be obtained. Hu et al. [34]used a solution chemistry and yielded flower-like brookite TiO2. The synthetic process has to undergo two key steps: (1) sodium hydroxide was first added to TiOSO4 solution to form a white precipitate, and (2) pH of the reaction solution was adjusted up to an alkaline environment at pH = 12.5 using different concentrations of nitric acid or sodium hydroxide, and thus the precipitate was transformed to flower-like brookite TiO2.

For anatase, sol-gel method is one of the most common routes for the sample synthesis [16]. Calcination temperature and periods of time determine the particle size and crystal structure, because the growth of nanoparticles is often accompanied by the change of crystal structure. At lower temperature, there is no phase transformation. Wu et al. found that the dried gel powder prepared by sol-gel method could transform to anatase phase with a particle size of 15 nm after calcination at 500 °C for 2 h [35]. High-purity spherical anatase with a size of only 7.7 nm was also synthesized near room temperature by a modified sol-gel method. For nanoscale anatase, water molecules are bonded to the surface of nanocrystal in forming hydration. Removing these hydration molecules could lead to the grains coarsen: without changing the crystal structure. Thus, the particles can grow up to a certain nanometer size by reducing the surface binding water [36]. The growth kinetics equation for anatase nanocrystals was determined by controlling the temperature and time [37]. Sugimoto et al. also synthesized uniform spindle-type particles by sol-gel method [38]. They found that the size of nanoparticles increased with pH, and triethanolamine can be used as a shape control agent. The shape of TiO2 nanoparticles changed from cubic to elliptical when pH was higher than 11 [39; 40].

Usually, the formation of rutile usually has to experience a high temperature calcination. For instance, after heating a titania gel powder at 900 °C for 4 h, rutile TiO2 at a particle size of 75 nm was obtained [35]. Comparing to other methods, hydrothermal condition [16] shows the merits of no need of high-temperature calcination, less agglomeration, uniform particle size distribution and high purity. Li et al. investigated the growth kinetics of rutile nanocrystals under hydrothermal conditions and revealed that the size of rutile nanocrystals increases obviously with the increase of reaction temperature or time, following a kinetic equation, Dn = k0 × t × e (
-Ea/RT) [41]. The morphological modulation of titanium dioxide nanomaterials during their synthesis has been a hot topic of research [22].

Both anatase and rutile have been prepared by varying the acidic media under hydrothermal method. Among given acidic media (like hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, sulphuric acid and acetic acid), hydrochloric acid is most favourable for the formation of rutile TiO2. Any changes in the concentration of hydrochloric acid not only affect the proportion of rutile phase in the final products, but also the particle size. Besides these, addition of sodium chloride to the solution also affects the phase composition and particle size [42].

TiO2 has a large morphology family, which includes spherical and spindle-shape, nanosheets, and nanotubes. Tight control of the experimental steps is key to chemical synthesis of nanocrystals of various shapes. For instance, as to obtain tubular anatase (about 8 nm in diameter and 100 nm in length), raw materials (either rutile or anatase) have to be treated with NaOH solution to form an amorphous product, and then nanotubes are produced during treatment with hydrochloric acid and distilled water [43; 44]. In fact, TiO2 nanoparticles with different shapes are primarily stabilized by surface adsorbents. These surface adsorption groups play an important role in crystal growth during chemical synthesis. Because interaction between adsorbents and surfaces can change the morphology and phase composition of nanocrystals. Theoretically, the phase transition from anatase to rutile can be caused by surface adsorption groups [45]. Li et al. prepared ultrathin anatase TiO2 nanosheets with a thickness of only 2.5 nm using an ethanol solvent thermal method [46]. Certainly, synthesis methods of TiO2 nanomaterials include many other methods, including direct oxidation, detonation, chemical vapor deposition, physical vapor deposition, and so on [47; 48; 49; 50]. Owing to strongly dependent on the morphology modulation, many types of TiO2 in different shapes have been explored for applications in photocatalysis and lithium batteries, which will be described in the section on applications of TiO2.

TiO2 H is another polymorph, totally different from any other crystalline structure of TiO2, the synthesis of TiO2 H relies on an unique structural transformation. Namely, precursor is first transform to hollandite-type KxTiO2 after calcination at high temperature in 5% H2/Ar. This hollandite intermediate is then treated with aqua regia solution to remove the potassium ions to yield TiO2 H [33]. Since high temperature sintering is required, one can only expect bulk TiO2 H, rather than nanoparticles [36].

It is universal for all oxide materials, that when the size of oxide particles is reduced to the nanoscale range, the phase transitions or thermodynamic properties would be different from the bulk counterparts, greatly affecting the practical applications [51]. Therefore, there have been great efforts in thermodynamic stability studies on TiO2 nanomaterials [24].

In 1998, Zhang and Banfield analyzed the phase stability of anatase and rutile nanoparticles thermodynamically by means of surface free energy and surface stress calculations. They proposed a critical dimension of 14 nm, below which anatase is more stable than rutile. This conclusion seems to be consistent with the experimental observation that rutile phase appears during the coarsening of anatase nanoparticles [21]. They further investigated the phase transformation and phase stability of TiO2 nanocrystals using samples consisting of anatase (5.1 nm) and rutile (8.1 nm). As temperature increases, anatase transforms first into brookite and/or rutile, and brookite transforms into rutile only at higher temperatures. The order of transformation between anatase and brookite is closely related to the initial particle size, and that changes in particle size not only alter the growth rate of polycrystalline oxides, but also affect the nanocrystal surface free energy. Small differences in surface free energy between anatase and brookite can reverse the phase transition sequence [26]. The phase transition is accompanied by the increase in grain sizes, and thus anatase is concluded most thermodynamically stable at sizes smaller than 11 nm, a dimension closer to that of 14 nm previously reported. Alternatively, in a larger size range of 11 - 35 nm, brookite is most stable, while beyond a dimension of 35 nm, the most stable phase is rutile [21; 26].

The phase stability of TiO2 is not only dependent on the particle size, but also on surface energy. By means of low temperature specific heat measurements, Wu et al. observed the differences in the heat capacity of TiO2 with different grain sizes, while for a larger size of 75 nm rutile, the heat capacity is very close to the bulk [35]. Since the smaller particle size is corresponding to a larger deformation, a higher surface free energy and internal stress, there is no clear and reasonable explanations for the anomaly in the heat capacity curve of 15 nm rutile TiO2 at high temperatures.

Polymorphs of TiO2 have been demonstrated to show a crossover in stability by calorimetric measurement. The surface enthalpy derived from calorimetric data follow a sequence: rutile (2.2 ± 0.2 J•m − 2), brookite (1.0 ± 0.2 J•m − 2), and anatase (0.4 ± 0.1 J•m − 2), which differ from that based on atomic simulation by Zhang et al., while the surface enthalpy variation pattern is consistent [23]. This realistic measurement can be combined with grain size and surface enthalpy to better understand the changes in phase stability of anatase and brookite as particle size varies [21]. To obtain accurate thermodynamic data for understanding stability, the influence of impurities has to be reduce so as to minimize the negative factors during the specific heat measurements. In the cases that certain organic impurities retained at sample surfaces could not be removed [52], experimental results for the presence of impurities have to be corrected, and the contribution of water is assumed to be surface physically adsorbed water when considering its contribution. Since this assumption is based on the weight loss of the sample below 673 K, not necessarily reasonable for bulk liquid water, the relevant explanations deserve further investigation.

For rutile and anatase at a particle size of 7 nm, Boerio-Goates et al. measured the specific heats by continuously reducing water content of samples. The calculated molar specific heats were all higher than those of the corresponding bulk counterparts. Since water has a large heat capacity, a higher content water for the nanoparticles would impose a greater effect on the specific heat results. The adsorption of water in nanomaterials not only makes a difference to the representation of chemical formula of target samples, but more importantly to the change in molecular weight during the calculation of specific heat. The molar specific heat of the anhydrous nanoparticles was estimated using the specific heat of water to be the same as the corresponding bulk TiO2 [20]. This also shows that the thermal stability of TiO2 nanoparticles is dominated by the surface adsorbed water, and that the coarsening of the nanocrystals is related to the surface water [37]. Similarly, Zhang et al. also found that water bound to the particles plays an important role in the structural transformation of ZnS [53].

In order to understand the effect of adsorbed water on the energy of TiO2 nanoparticles, Levchenko et al. carried out a detailed study using a custom-built Calvet twin microcalorimeter combined with Micromeritics ASAP 2010 gas adsorption system. They calculated the differential and integral enthalpies of water adsorption on surfaces of anatase and rutile nanocrystals, and considered the entropy of adsorption that reflects the mobility of adsorbed water on surface. Water on the nanocrystals surface is demonstrated not loose but tightly bound to the sample surface, and water content for rutile surface is higher in the same specific surface area range. The water adsorbed on TiO2 surface has a high surface free energy, and therefore the fluidity of water is highly limited, which leads to a decrease in adsorption entropy and free energy. When these changed energy parameters were supplemented in the calculation of calorimetric surface enthalpies of rutile and anatase nanocrystals, the final experimental results are different from those obtained by Ranade et al. who assumed surface absorbed water to be liquid water. Such thermodynamic data that take into account impurities such as adsorbed water, could be more accurate, which support the energy crossover of polymorphs TiO2 proposed previously [21; 23; 52].

The presence of water on surfaces of anatase nanoparticles is further confirmed by inelastic neutron scattering to be in the form of tightly bound water, rather than flowing water [54]. The adsorption of water molecules on nanoparticle surface is inevitable due to the unsaturated coordination of titanium ions on nanoparticle surfaces. Similarly, water molecules are bound to Ti ions with low coordination numbers and significant distortion occurred around Ti ions for rutile nanoparticles, resulting in a lattice expansion and enhanced covalency. A surface defect dipole model explains these anomalous physical phenomena well [55]. A theoretical model of water adsorption on the surfaces of titanium dioxide nanoparticles is also proposed to explain the thermodynamic data [24]. The surface defect interactions for rutile could be strengthened by co-doping trivalent and pentavalent cations to give rise to giant dielectric constants, which could be reasonably explained in terms of electron-pinned defect-dipoles model [56].

With the development in experimental accesses to TiO2 of different phases, important thermodynamic data have been reported for rutile, anatase, brookite, TiO2-B and TiO2 H after correction for water adsorption on nanocrystal surface. These thermodynamic results are important for understanding the crystal structure, phase stability, and crystal defects of TiO2 [36; 57].

Much more thermodynamic data have been reported on TiO2. The initial ones came from the thermodynamic data reported in literature for anatase and rutile that take into account the bare nanoparticles and the contributions from surface adsorbed water, carbon dioxide and some unknown impurity plates. Several experimental results were simply corrected that assumes surface absorbed water molecules as liquid water [52]. The lowest temperature for early specific heat measurements of TiO2 nanocrystals is set at 78 K [35]. Now, the testing temperature can reached as low as 0.5 K [58; 59]. Such a wide temperature specific heat measurement helps to fully understand the thermodynamic stability of TiO2 polymorphs and the energy crossover [36]. Beside the measurement temperature range, specific heat measurement methods are necessarily improved to obtain accurate thermodynamic data at low temperatures. Shi et al. developed a method of specific heat measurements for metal oxide nanocrystals using small copper cups [60; 61].

For rutile and anatase, specific heat measurements on nano-TiO2 and bulk-TiO2 were performed at temperature down to 0.5 K, and the specific heat fitting at lower temperatures is relatively complicate [58; 59]. Before 2016, thermodynamic data of brookite TiO2 were rarely reported in literature, except for those first obtained for synthetic plate-titania-type TiO2 using a quantum-designed physical property measurement system [31].

Recently, the specific heat data of brookite, TiO2-B, and TiO2 H, these polymorphs measured in a temperature range from 1.9 to 300 K [31; 32; 33] were analyzed in three sections separately: (i) the data in a low temperature range is fitted to a sum of theoretical functions, (ⅱ) orthogonal polynomial is used to fits the data in the medium temperature range, and (iii) a combination of Einstein and Debye functions is adopted for the data in the high temperature range. This data analysis gives thermodynamic information on the specific heat data, standard molar entropy, molar enthalpy, and Gibbs free energy of the samples [58; 59]. Detailed thermodynamic data for rutile, anatase, brookite, TiO2-B, and TiO2 H at a temperature of 298.15 K and a pressure of 0.1 MPa are listed in Table 3. Although the average particle size of TiO2 H is in a few microns, the thermodynamic properties of anhydrous rutile and anatase nanocrystals are equal to those of the corresponding bulk TiO2. It is thus reasonable to compare the thermodynamic properties of rutile and anatase nanoparticles to other polymorphs at larger particle sizes. A comparison of the Gibbs free energy of the three common crystal structures of TiO2 reveals that the thermal stability of brookite is significantly better than that of anatase and rutile. Combining the Gibbs free energy of TiO2 - H and TiO2 - B at room temperature listed in Table 3, a stability order of TiO2 polymorphs is, anatase<rutile<TiO2
H<TiO2-B<brookite. There are many similarities in literature with regard to the specific heat data for these five crystalline structures of TiO2. For instance, the specific heat value of TiO2-B is similar to that of brookite over the entire temperature range, but significantly higher above 200 K than other three polymorphs of TiO2. For TiO2 H is similar to both rutile and anatase. The change in specific heat is somewhat associated with the symmetry variations for these phase structures. For the latter cases, rutile, anatase and TiO2 H all have a tetragonal phase, while the other two phases (orthorhombic and monoclinic phases) have a much less symmetry. Lowering the symmetry can give rise to increased vibrational modes relative to the high symmetry phase structures, beneficial for increases in lattice specific heat [31; 32; 33; 58; 59] (Tables 2
, 4 ).

TiO2 plays an important role in solving energy and environmental problems. In this section, several recent progresses for TiO2 of different phases in the fields of photocatalysis and lithium batteries are briefly summarized.

Since the first report on photocatalytic splitting of water on TiO2 electrodes, there have been extensive studies on photocatalysts [62], such as from photocatalytic production of hydrogen to degradation of organic pollutants and to reduction of carbon dioxide [18; 63; 64]. Among five polymorphs of TiO2 described in previous section, rutile and anatase are most studied for photocatalysis [25], in which anatase shows a photocatalytic activity superior to that of rutile [65]. Such a difference is closely related to crystal structure, crystallinity, particle size, and so on [66]. In this regard, the regulation of particle size could be highly important that determines specific surface area and active sites available for photocatalytic reactions [67]. Particle size is also one of the dominant factors that determine the thermodynamic stability of TiO2. For instance, rutile is the thermodynamically stable phase at macroscopic scale, while anatase is stable at nanoscale [23]. Further, both particle size and phase structures contribute to the band gap of TiO2: Smaller particle size usually give larger band gap, while phase transition from anatase to rutile leads to a slight decrease in bandgap from 3.2 eV to 3.0 eV. The smaller band gap could extend photoresponse from ultraviolet to the visible light region, increasing the light energy utilization of sunlight, but also facilitates the jump of electrons from valence band to conduction band in forming photogenerated electron-hole pairs [68; 69]. During photocatalytic reaction, these electrons and holes will undergo redox reactions to become highly reactive radicals, the key to the degradation of organic pollutants [18].

Polymorphs of TiO2 have shown distinct properties in photocatalytic overall water splitting: For rutile, hydrogen and oxygen can be ultimately yielded, while only hydrogen is produced for anatase and brookite [65]. Such a difference is caused by both kinetic and thermodynamic factors. In terms of kinetics, rutile is less active in photoreduction of oxygen when compared to anatase, which means that rutile favors the overall water splitting reaction, in particular by depositing platinum nanoparticles [70; 71] or changing the nanorod aspect ratio [72]. Although there were significant differences in the performance of these catalysts in the overall water splitting performance, all of these catalysts had relatively low catalytic reaction efficiency. Mixing anatase and rutile (such as P25) is an effective way to improve photocatalytic activity, because of the enhanced transfer rate of photo-excited electrons at rutile-anatase interface [68; 73; 74]. Furthermore, by constructing an interface between TiO2 and other semiconductors can be improved photocatalytic performance greatly [75; 76]. Traditionally, it is widely accepted that brookite TiO2 is photocatalytic inert. A high-quality brookite TiO2 single-crystalline nanosheets surrounded with {210},{101} and {201} facets exhibit outstanding activity toward the catalytic degradation of organic contaminants superior even to that of Degussa P25 [77]. In addition, the photocatalytic activity of brookite TiO2 can also be improved by targeted deposition of ZnO2 [78]. Therefore, control over the particle size and phase composition of TiO2 allows one to better understand the thermodynamic contributions to the photocatalytic performance [79], even though the impacts of special morphologies (like exposed surfaces, nanosheets, or highly ordered nanotube arrays) could not be ignored [46; 80; 81; 82].

Polymorphs of TiO2 could be structurally modified to enhance the photocatalytic performance (including hydrogen production, carbon dioxide reduction or degradation of organic matter) [17; 64; 83]. Point defects are one of the simplest forms of defect engineering and can generally be achieved by elemental doping and hydrogen reduction treatments. When reduced by hydrogen, rutile TiO2 shows a photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity exceeding to P25 [84]. Similar cases have been reported for metal doping in rutile [85] or in anatase [86] and for non-metallic element doping [87; 88; 89].

Taking photocatalytic reaction as example, either photosynthesis or photoreduction involves a series of electron transfer steps to convert the sunlight energy into the chemical energy [90]. To well understand electron transfer reaction, it is necessary to study not just its kinetics, but also thermodynamics. However, in contrast to the kinetics of electron transfer mechanisms, such as, the lifetime of electron at excite state, electron transfer speed, the separation efficiency of photogenerated carrier, etc., thermodynamic information is far less accessible. Thermodynamics reveals the energy levels of reactants, products and catalyst, as well as the driving forces in the reaction.

Thermodynamics reveals the energy levels of reactants, products and catalyst, as well as the driving forces in the reaction. The driving force of the chemical reaction is the Gibbs free energy (G), which is composed of enthalpic (H) and entropic (S) components. Gibbs free energy (G = H – TS) also dominate the stability of materials in application. For example, the Gibbs free energy of anatase TiO2 is −6.19KJ/mol, higher than rutile TiO2 [23; 32]. In the photocatalytic reaction, the better performance of anatase TiO2 could deterioration due to the structure transformation from anatase to rutile under long time light irradiation. However, there is limited research on the relationship between thermodynamic and energy applications for titanium dioxide, which hinders the practical application of functional materials.

Among all polymorphs, rutile, anatase and TiO2-B have been proved potential for uses as anode of lithium-ion batteries [19]. For both anatase and rutile, particles with smaller sizes usually give an electrochemical performance better than the micron counterpart [91] [92]. For hollandite-type TiO2 and brookite, micron size in nature, phase structure plays a very important role in determining the insertion behavior of lithium [29; 93]. Micron-sized TiO2-B can accommodate more lithium than any other polymorphs. Nanoscale TiO2-B possesses a better performance than the bulk. Nanotubes and nanowires could also be applicable for lithium batteries [28; 94]. Several strategies for TiO2-B nanowires (e.g., compounding with redox graphene, changing the nanowire phase composition, or fabricating porous nanosheet structure) have been discovered to further enhance the performance of lithium-ion batteries by greatly facilitating ion transport and electrode response [95; 96; 97]. Porous structures such as in hollow microspheres, cubic microboxes, and crystal structure are also found dominated for anatase and rutile [98; 99; 100; 101]. The construction of porous nanostructures has a significant effect on the specific capacity, long term stability and multiplicative performance of lithium batteries.

TiO2 represents a model material with one simple composition but a variety of different phases, which allows one to better understand the dependence of thermodynamic properties on polymorphs structure. This review summarizes recent advances in polymorphs, experimental accesses, thermodynamic stabilities, thermodynamic data and applications for TiO2. More complete thermodynamic studies of TiO2 reveal that the energy differences between nanoparticle and the bulk samples are due to water molecules tightly bonded to surface defect sites, so that differences in surface energy become critical to stabilizing TiO2 nanophases. This review emphasizes the importance of considering the impact of polymorphic structure on thermodynamic properties, which may have broad implications for the prediction of material functionalities in application-critical analogues of TiO2 polymorphs.

It is well established that entropy and enthalpy, two key thermodynamic parameters, are the driving forces for chemical reactions, while their imperfect cancelation governs the biophysical characteristics of most organisms. For inorganic materials, entropy and enthalpy directly determine thermodynamic properties and stability during practical operations. Thus, precise measurements of specific heat, entropy, and enthalpy will be highly useful in determining entropy and enthalpy changes under as a function of external conditions (like temperature or pressure), which are essential for improving the performance of functional materials. Configurational entropy in high entropy materials has received a lot of interest in recent years [6], However, thermodynamic properties such as specific heat and enthalpy, which determine the stability of materials, have not received enough attention in energy application. As a result, this is a critical feature of titanium dioxide that need to be considered in future research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSFC 22175070, 21771075, 21871106, and U20A20246.
